programs.name: Aged and Disabled (AD) HCBS Waiver
programs.tagline: Helps your parent stay home with services like personal care, home modifications, and meal delivery instead of nursing home care.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs significant daily help with bathing, dressing, or other basic activities, the AD HCBS Waiver can provide home-based services to keep them out of a nursing home. The program covers personal care, home modifications, meal delivery, adult day health, and respite care — all at no cost to families who qualify.

To qualify, your parent must be 65+ (or any age with a disability), meet Nebraska Medicaid income limits, and pass a clinical assessment showing they need nursing home-level care. Because the assessment is detailed and strict, most applicants need help with at least 3 activities of daily living plus additional health or cognitive issues.

The Level of Care assessment gets scheduled within 14 days of applying. Home equity cannot exceed $752,000, though your parent can keep their home if they live there or plan to return.

summary: Age 65+ or any age with disability
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home-level care — typically requires significant limitations with at least 3 activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, transferring, mobility) plus health conditions or cognitive issues

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through iServe Nebraska or call 877-667-6266 — Level of Care assessment scheduled within 14 days.

steps.title: Confirm Medicaid eligibility
steps.description: Your parent must already receive Nebraska Medicaid. If not, apply for Medicaid first at iServe Nebraska or call 877-667-6266.

steps.title: Submit waiver application
steps.description: Apply online at iServe Nebraska, call 877-667-6266, or email DHHS.HCBSWaiverApp@nebraska.gov. Include medical records showing care needs.

steps.title: Complete Level of Care assessment
steps.description: A clinical assessor will evaluate your parent's daily living limitations using the interRAI Home Care tool. This determines if they need nursing home-level care.

steps.title: Receive service plan
steps.description: If approved, DHHS creates an individualized service plan based on assessed needs and available waiver services.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Level of Care assessment scheduled within 14 days of application submission
applicationGuide.tip: Gather detailed medical records before applying — the assessment focuses on specific daily living limitations, not general health status.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Life insurance with face value under $1,500
Home if spouse, minor child, or disabled child lives there

categories.name: Medical
items: Records of falls or emergency incidents

contentSections.text: This waiver expires July 31, 2026. Verify current status with DHHS at 877-667-6266 as renewal may be pending.

The Level of Care assessment is strict — your parent must demonstrate significant limitations with multiple daily activities, not just general health problems. Be specific about what help they need with bathing, dressing, toileting, and mobility.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for the waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as her home equity is under $752,000. Home equity is the current value minus what she owes on the mortgage. The limit doesn't apply if you or another family member lives in the home.

faqs.question: What if Dad needs help with only one or two daily activities?
faqs.answer: He likely won't qualify. The Level of Care assessment typically requires significant limitations with at least 3 activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, transferring, mobility) plus additional health conditions or cognitive issues.

faqs.question: How much personal care will the waiver cover each week?
faqs.answer: Hours vary based on individual assessment and need. DHHS creates a personalized service plan after the Level of Care evaluation. Contact 877-667-6266 for details about typical service levels.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom's income is slightly too high for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: She won't qualify for the waiver since Nebraska Medicaid eligibility is required first. However, medical expenses can sometimes reduce countable income. Call 877-667-6266 to discuss her specific situation.

faqs.question: Can we choose which services Dad receives from the waiver?
faqs.answer: Services are based on assessed need, not preference. The evaluation determines what level of personal care, home modifications, meal delivery, or other services are medically necessary for his specific limitations.

faqs.question: Is there a waiting list for waiver services in Nebraska?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify current waitlist status. Call DHHS at 877-667-6266 for up-to-date information about service availability in your area.

programs.name: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: All medical care, medications, and home services under one program for parents who need nursing home-level care but want to stay home.

localEntities.name: Immanuel Pathways, Omaha (serving Eastern Nebraska counties)

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay at home, PACE provides everything under one roof: all medical care, medications, transportation to appointments, home care services, and adult day programs. Because PACE coordinates all care through one team, your parent won't juggle multiple doctors, insurance claims, or copays — the program covers it all.

PACE is only available in eastern Nebraska counties (Douglas, Sarpy, and select areas of Cass, Dodge, Saunders, and Washington). Your parent must be 55+ and pass a state assessment proving they need nursing facility-level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility). There are no income or asset limits to join, but Medicaid eligibility determines whether it's free or requires monthly premiums.

summary: Lives in eastern Nebraska service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Nebraska as needing nursing facility level of care (requires assessment showing need for daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Lives in Douglas, Sarpy, or select zip codes in Cass, Dodge, Saunders, or Washington Counties
Able to live safely in community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage

applicationGuide.summary: Contact Immanuel Pathways directly for eligibility screening and enrollment — they handle the entire process including state assessments.

steps.title: Call Immanuel Pathways
steps.description: Contact the only PACE provider in Nebraska to schedule an eligibility assessment

steps.title: Complete nursing facility assessment
steps.description: State assessor evaluates whether your parent needs nursing home-level care

steps.title: Verify safe community living
steps.description: PACE team determines if your parent can live safely at home with their services

steps.title: Submit required documents
steps.description: Provide ID, medical records, residency proof, and Medicare/Medicaid information

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has Medicare Advantage, they must disenroll before joining PACE — PACE becomes their sole Medicare and Medicaid provider.

contentSections.heading: Medicaid Coverage Income Limits
contentSections.footnote: 2025 limits for free PACE coverage. Higher incomes can still join but pay monthly premiums.

offices.name: Immanuel Pathways Eastern Nebraska
offices.address: Omaha area serving Douglas, Sarpy, and select zip codes in Cass, Dodge, Saunders, Washington Counties

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of address in service area

categories.name: Financial
items: Bank statements (if applying for Medicaid coverage)

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in eastern Nebraska counties around Omaha. If your parent lives elsewhere in Nebraska, they cannot access this program.
Private pay for PACE can cost $7,000+ per month if your parent doesn't qualify for Medicaid. Get the Medicaid assessment first to determine actual costs.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she joins PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE is designed to help people stay in their homes instead of moving to nursing facilities. The primary home doesn't count toward asset limits for Medicaid coverage.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is too high for free PACE?
faqs.answer: He can still join PACE but will pay monthly premiums. About 90% of PACE participants qualify for Medicaid coverage, but private pay is available for those with higher incomes.

faqs.question: Does PACE replace Dad's current Medicare plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE becomes your parent's sole Medicare and Medicaid provider. They cannot have Medicare Advantage or other Medicare plans while enrolled. However, they can leave PACE anytime and return to original Medicare.

faqs.question: How do I know if Mom needs 'nursing facility level of care'?
faqs.answer: Nebraska will assess whether she needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, mobility, or medication management. If she would qualify for nursing home admission based on these needs, she likely meets the requirement.

faqs.question: What happens if we move outside the service area?
faqs.answer: Your parent must live in Douglas, Sarpy, or select areas of Cass, Dodge, Saunders, or Washington Counties. Moving outside this area means losing PACE eligibility.

faqs.question: Can Mom still see her current doctors with PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE uses its own care team and providers. Your parent will get new doctors through the PACE program, but the team coordinates all care to avoid the confusion of multiple providers.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: Help paying Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays for parents with limited income.
programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford Medicare costs, Nebraska's Medicare Savings Program can pay their Medicare Part B premiums ($185/month in 2026), plus deductibles and copays depending on which tier they qualify for. The program has three levels: QMB covers the most costs, while SLMB and QI focus on premium assistance.

Your parent must have Medicare Part A eligibility and meet income limits ranging from $1,350/month (QMB) to $1,660/month (QI) for single individuals. Because this is administered through Nebraska's Medicaid system, asset limits also apply — from $9,090 to $9,950 for individuals depending on the program tier.

Qualifying for the QI tier automatically enrolls your parent in Extra Help for prescription drug costs, which many families don't realize is an additional benefit.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums ($2,220/year) plus deductibles and copays for QMB tier

otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A (even if not enrolled)
Must be US citizen or qualified non-citizen

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through ACCESSNebraska (Nebraska's Medicaid agency) with processing within 45 days.

steps.title: Contact ACCESSNebraska
steps.description: Call or visit ACCESSNebraska to request a Medicare Savings Program application form.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect recent pay stubs or Social Security statements, bank statements, and proof of Medicare eligibility.

steps.title: Submit completed application
steps.description: Return the application with all required documentation to ACCESSNebraska.

steps.title: Wait for determination
steps.description: QMB eligibility starts the first day of the month after approval. SLMB and QI can be retroactive up to three months.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Within 45 days for QMB; SLMB and QI may be retroactive up to three months
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the year since income limits change every April — last year's qualification doesn't guarantee this year's.

rows.description: Pays Part B premiums, deductibles, copays
Pays Part B premiums + Extra Help for prescriptions

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of US citizenship or qualified non-citizen status
Medicare card or proof of Medicare eligibility

categories.name: Financial
items: Recent pay stubs or Social Security statements

contentSections.text: Income limits change every April. If your parent was denied last year, they may qualify this year with the updated thresholds.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary residence and one vehicle are exempt from the asset limits and won't count against qualification.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the QMB limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify for SLMB (up to $1,478/month) or QI (up to $1,660/month). These programs still cover Medicare Part B premiums, saving $2,220/year.

faqs.question: Does this affect her other benefits?
faqs.answer: No, Medicare Savings Programs don't reduce Social Security or other benefits. If she qualifies for QI, she automatically gets Extra Help with prescription drug costs too.

faqs.question: When do the benefits start?
faqs.answer: For QMB, benefits start the first day of the month after approval. SLMB and QI can be retroactive up to three months before the application date if she was eligible.

faqs.question: What happens if she needs nursing home care later?
faqs.answer: The Medicare Savings Program only covers Medicare costs. For nursing home care, you'd need to explore Medicaid separately, though some of the financial information may transfer.

faqs.question: Do we need to reapply every year?
faqs.answer: The program requires periodic renewals, but you don't need to reapply annually. However, income limits change each April, so it's worth checking if she becomes eligible in a new year.
